refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits during agreed network conditions.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting packet loss quality criteria. The refined FR isolates the quality/performance obligation (communication integrity) as the core functional requirement, removing the implicit 'support teleoperation' which is a higher-level system capability. The phrase 'communication quality meeting the customer-defined packet loss limit' is vague and prescriptive; 'maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits' is solution-neutral and measurable. The addition of 'during agreed network conditions' acknowledges the constraint context without embedding it as a design assumption.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation sessions across agreed network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles) and measure end-to-end packet loss for command and feedback channels. Verify that measured packet loss does not exceed the customer-defined threshold (e.g., ≤1%) over a statistically significant sample period. Document network conditions and acceptance criteria in the test plan.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit teleoperation commands from the tele-operator interface to the remote equipment with fidelity sufficient to preserve operator intent.

FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit teleoperation feedback (sensor data, equipment state, video) from the remote equipment to the tele-operator interface with fidelity sufficient to enable safe and effective remote operation.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The NGHE solution shall detect and report communication degradation (packet loss, latency, signal quality) to the tele-operator and safety manager in real time.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time network quality metrics (loss rate, latency variance) to maintain command/feedback integrity within customer-defined thresholds.
DP2: Establish a dual-channel communication architecture with independent network paths and automatic failover logic to ensure continuous teleoperation control availability.

DP3: Deploy application-level forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy ratios, tuned to customer network conditions during commissioning, to absorb packet loss without requiring retransmission.

DP4: Implement a prioritized message queue with quality-of-service (QoS) differentiation, ensuring critical control commands are transmitted with higher reliability guarantees than non-critical telemetry.
DP5: Establish a closed-loop acknowledgment and timeout-driven selective repeat protocol at the application layer, with adaptive retry windows calibrated to measured round-trip latency.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP4 (Composite): Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time network quality metrics, combined with prioritized message queuing and QoS differentiation. Critical teleoperation commands (e.g., emergency stop, motion control) receive higher redundancy and acknowledgment guarantees; non-critical telemetry (e.g., sensor readings) use lighter-weight encoding. Redundancy ratios and retry policies are configurable and tuned during commissioning to meet customer-defined packet loss acceptance criteria (≤1% or customer target) under agreed network conditions.

potential_sources: Moderate coupling between adaptive redundancy logic (DP1) and QoS prioritization (DP4): both depend on real-time network state estimation and message classification. Mitigation: isolate network state observer and message classifier as shared utilities; define clear interfaces between redundancy engine and QoS scheduler.

Weak coupling to worksite safety plan: QoS prioritization must respect safety-critical command ordering (e.g., emergency stop always highest priority). Mitigation: safety policy constraints are inputs to QoS configuration, not design logic.

Potential coupling to customer network conditions: redundancy ratios and retry windows must be tunable without code changes. Mitigation: externalize all thresholds and timing parameters to configuration files or runtime APIs.
coupling_check.notes: The composite DP avoids tight coupling by separating concerns: network quality sensing (shared), message prioritization (policy-driven), and redundancy application (algorithm-driven). Each can be evolved independently if interfaces remain stable. No single-point-of-failure dependency identified.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer network conditions are measurable and stable during agreed operational windows. RISK: If network conditions change unpredictably (e.g., interference, congestion spikes), pre-tuned redundancy ratios may become insufficient. MITIGATION: Include runtime network quality monitoring and alerting; design for online recalibration or graceful degradation.

ASSUMPTION: Packet loss is the dominant failure mode; latency and jitter are within acceptable bounds. RISK: If latency becomes excessive (e.g., >500ms round-trip), retransmission-based recovery may introduce unacceptable control delays. MITIGATION: Define latency acceptance criteria explicitly; consider hybrid FEC+retransmission approach if latency is variable.

ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation commands and feedback can be classified into priority tiers without ambiguity. RISK: Incorrect prioritization (e.g., non-critical sensor data marked as critical) may waste redundancy budget or delay important information. MITIGATION: Establish clear message taxonomy and validation rules during commissioning.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined packet loss target (≤1%) is achievable with reasonable redundancy overhead (e.g., <20% additional bandwidth). RISK: If target is extremely stringent (e.g., <0.1%) or network baseline loss is high (e.g., >5%), redundancy overhead may become prohibitive. MITIGATION: Validate feasibility during network assessment phase; establish fallback policies (e.g., operator notification, reduced autonomy mode).

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan does not mandate real-time guarantees beyond what adaptive redundancy can provide. RISK: If safety-critical commands require deterministic latency bounds, adaptive retry logic may violate safety requirements. MITIGATION: Coordinate with safety engineering; define maximum acceptable latency for each command class.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity by ensuring that critical control commands (as defined in the worksite safety plan) are delivered with ≤1% packet loss (or customer-defined target) and maximum latency of [T_max] milliseconds under agreed network conditions; non-critical telemetry may tolerate higher loss rates if explicitly approved by the customer. DP shall be configurable and tunable during commissioning without code changes, and shall include runtime monitoring and operator alerting if network conditions degrade beyond acceptable thresholds.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns adaptive packet redundancy, selective retransmission logic, prioritized message queuing, QoS differentiation, and configurable redundancy tuning for teleoperation commands and telemetry streams. Manages real-time network quality metrics assessment and applies differentiated encoding/retry policies based on message criticality and customer-defined packet loss targets.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw network quality metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss rate); sends redundantly-encoded packets with retry policies applied

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives high-priority teleoperation commands (emergency stop, motion control) for critical-path redundancy and acknowledgment guarantees

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Sends sensor telemetry with lighter-weight encoding and reduced redundancy; receives telemetry ingestion requests to prioritize queue

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals safety-critical delivery failures or network degradation that exceeds customer packet loss acceptance criteria

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Teleoperation is the natural owner of command prioritization, redundancy tuning, and QoS differentiation for remote control. Network Link interface provides metrics; Tasking and Telemetry interfaces align with command/telemetry streams. Minor diffusion: Safety threshold enforcement may require SafetyAlert coordination, but logic remains localized.

Moderate fit: Safety Services could own packet loss monitoring and threshold enforcement, but would not naturally own the adaptive redundancy algorithm, message queuing, or QoS encoding logic. High diffusion: Teleoperation would still need to implement retry/redundancy independently, creating duplication and coupling.

Poor fit: Vehicle module typically owns vehicle state, actuation, and sensor fusion—not network protocol logic. Placing adaptive redundancy here creates tight coupling between vehicle dynamics and network transport, violating separation of concerns. High diffusion: Teleoperation and Safety Services would both need to interface with Vehicle for redundancy policies.

Weak fit: NGHE Solution is likely a top-level system container. Placing DP1+DP4 here avoids false specificity but sacrifices ownership clarity and makes testing/reuse difficult. Diffusion: Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Safety Services would all depend on NGHE for redundancy policies, creating circular dependencies.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the most specific and cohesive boundary for DP1+DP4. It naturally owns the logic for differentiating critical commands (emergency stop, motion control) from non-critical telemetry, applying adaptive redundancy and retry policies, and tuning QoS based on network metrics. The module receives Tasking (high-priority commands) and Telemetry (sensor streams) as inputs, consumes Network Link metrics, and signals SafetyAlert when packet loss exceeds customer thresholds. This containment minimizes diffusion: redundancy logic does not leak into Vehicle (which remains agnostic to transport), and Safety Services remains focused on safety rule enforcement rather than protocol tuning.
containment_risks: Risk: Network Link interface may not expose sufficient granularity of real-time metrics (e.g., per-flow latency, jitter variance) needed for adaptive tuning. Mitigation: Ensure Network Link contract includes detailed QoS telemetry fields.

Risk: Tasking and Telemetry interfaces may not clearly delineate message criticality (e.g., which commands are emergency-stop vs. routine). Mitigation: Extend Tasking and Telemetry contracts to include priority/criticality metadata.

Risk: Customer-defined packet loss acceptance criteria (≤1% or custom target) may need to be stored/updated at runtime. Mitigation: Ensure Teleoperation has access to a configuration store or SafetyAlert provides threshold updates.

Risk: If Vehicle module also needs to apply redundancy to internal sensor streams or actuator feedback, logic may duplicate. Mitigation: Clarify whether Vehicle owns only vehicle-internal redundancy; Teleoperation owns only remote command/telemetry redundancy.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP1+DP4 is necessary. The DP is well-scoped and logically cohesive. However, to strengthen containment, recommend clarifying the Tasking and Telemetry interface contracts to include explicit priority/criticality fields (e.g., 'CRITICAL', 'NORMAL', 'LOW') so that Teleoperation can deterministically apply redundancy policies without inferring intent. Additionally, ensure Network Link exposes per-packet or per-flow QoS metrics (loss rate, latency, jitter) rather than aggregate statistics, enabling real-time adaptive tuning.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. Packet loss acceptance criterion (≤1% or customer-defined target) is measurable and testable under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can emit telemetry on actual packet loss rates achieved, enabling direct comparison against acceptance criteria. SafetyAlert interface provides explicit signal path for threshold violations. Commissioning tuning phase allows empirical validation of redundancy ratios and retry policies against customer targets.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. FR requirement is fully allocated to Teleoperation IM boundary. The module owns end-to-end responsibility for maintaining communication integrity: (1) consumes Network Link metrics to assess real-time quality, (2) applies adaptive redundancy and selective retransmission logic, (3) differentiates message criticality via Tasking/Telemetry interfaces, (4) signals SafetyAlert on threshold breach. No ambiguity regarding which module is accountable for meeting the ≤1% packet loss target.

diffusion_risks: MINOR: SafetyAlert coordination. Teleoperation must signal when packet loss exceeds customer thresholds, but Safety Services owns safety rule enforcement. Risk of logic duplication if Safety Services independently monitors packet loss. Mitigation: Establish clear contract—Teleoperation reports raw packet loss metrics; Safety Services interprets against safety policies. Teleoperation does not enforce safety decisions.

MINOR: Vehicle module internal redundancy. If Vehicle also applies redundancy to internal sensor streams or actuator feedback loops, algorithm logic may duplicate across Teleoperation and Vehicle. Mitigation: Clarify scope—Teleoperation owns remote command/telemetry redundancy; Vehicle owns only vehicle-internal transport (if any). Document boundary explicitly in interface contracts.

MODERATE: Configuration store access. Customer-defined packet loss acceptance criteria and redundancy tuning parameters must be updatable at runtime. If configuration is scattered across multiple stores (e.g., SafetyAlert thresholds, Teleoperation tuning parameters, Network Link QoS policies), diffusion risk increases. Mitigation: Centralize configuration in a single store accessible to Teleoperation; SafetyAlert reads thresholds from same store to avoid inconsistency.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT (0.92 containment score). Teleoperation is the natural and cohesive owner of DP1+DP4. The module's responsibilities align tightly with the DP logic: (1) Adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission are core teleoperation transport concerns, not vehicle dynamics or safety rule enforcement. (2) Prioritized message queuing and QoS differentiation directly map to the distinction between critical commands (emergency stop, motion control) and non-critical telemetry. (3) Configurable redundancy tuning during commissioning is a Teleoperation-owned calibration activity. (4) Real-time network quality metrics assessment is naturally co-located with the redundancy logic that consumes those metrics. External interfaces (Network Link, Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and minimize coupling. No evidence of DP logic leaking into Vehicle (which remains agnostic to transport) or Safety Services (which remains focused on safety rule enforcement). Diffusion score of 0.08 reflects only minor coordination overhead with SafetyAlert, which is acceptable.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1+DP4 (composite) directly and sufficiently addresses FR requirement. FR demands: (1) maintain teleoperation communication integrity, (2) within customer-defined packet loss limits (≤1% or target), (3) during agreed network conditions. DP provides: (1) adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission to maintain integrity, (2) configurable redundancy ratios and retry policies tuned during commissioning to meet customer packet loss targets, (3) real-time network quality metrics assessment to adapt to agreed network conditions. DP is logically sound, implementable, and testable. No logical gaps or contradictions. DP correctly distinguishes critical commands (emergency stop, motion control) from non-critical telemetry, applying proportionate redundancy to each. Composite structure (DP1 + DP4) is justified: DP1 handles adaptive redundancy/retransmission; DP4 handles prioritized queuing/QoS differentiation. Both are necessary and non-redundant.

recommended_revisions: INTERFACE CONTRACT ENHANCEMENT: Extend Tasking interface contract to include explicit priority/criticality metadata (e.g., enum: CRITICAL, NORMAL, LOW or boolean: is_safety_critical). This enables Teleoperation to deterministically apply redundancy policies without inferring intent from command type. Example: emergency_stop command tagged CRITICAL receives max redundancy; routine sensor query tagged LOW receives minimal redundancy.

INTERFACE CONTRACT ENHANCEMENT: Extend Telemetry interface contract to include priority/criticality field for telemetry streams. Allows Teleoperation to apply differentiated encoding and queue prioritization based on telemetry importance (e.g., critical sensor readings receive higher redundancy; diagnostic logs receive lighter encoding).

INTERFACE CONTRACT ENHANCEMENT: Ensure Network Link interface exposes per-packet or per-flow QoS metrics (packet loss rate, latency, jitter, variance) rather than aggregate statistics. Real-time adaptive tuning requires granular, current-state metrics. Define update frequency (e.g., every 100ms) and metric precision (e.g., loss rate as percentage with 0.1% resolution).

CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT: Establish a centralized configuration store for customer-defined packet loss acceptance criteria, redundancy ratios, and retry policies. Teleoperation reads from this store at startup and subscribes to runtime updates. SafetyAlert also reads thresholds from same store to ensure consistency. Document configuration schema and update protocol.

SAFETY ALERT COORDINATION: Clarify contract between Teleoperation and SafetyAlert. Teleoperation reports raw packet loss metrics and threshold breaches; SafetyAlert interprets against safety policies and decides on safety actions (e.g., emergency stop, operator alert). Teleoperation does not enforce safety decisions—it only signals conditions.

VEHICLE BOUNDARY CLARIFICATION: Document whether Vehicle module owns any internal redundancy logic (e.g., for sensor fusion or actuator feedback). If yes, establish clear boundary: Teleoperation owns remote command/telemetry redundancy; Vehicle owns vehicle-internal transport redundancy. If no, confirm Vehicle remains agnostic to transport protocols.

COMMISSIONING PROCEDURE: Define commissioning workflow for tuning redundancy ratios and retry policies to meet customer packet loss targets. Include: (1) baseline network condition characterization, (2) iterative tuning of redundancy parameters, (3) validation against acceptance criteria under agreed conditions, (4) documentation of final tuning parameters in configuration store.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR-DP-IM alignment with HIGH verification feasibility and EXCELLENT (0.92) containment. However, the MODERATE diffusion risk around configuration store access, combined with HIGH safety criticality of the system, warrants decomposition of the Teleoperation IM into sub-level FRs to explicitly address: (1) configuration management and consistency across SafetyAlert/Teleoperation boundaries, (2) real-time network quality metric acquisition and adaptation logic, (3) prioritized message queuing with criticality-aware redundancy application, and (4) safety alert signaling protocol. These sub-FRs will force explicit architectural decisions on configuration ownership, metric freshness, and safety coordination—critical for high-safety systems. Recommended revisions (interface contracts, configuration management, safety coordination) are architectural in nature and require sub-level decomposition to be properly realized and verified.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-TEL-1: Acquire and maintain real-time network quality metrics (packet loss rate, latency, jitter) from Network Link with defined update frequency and precision to enable adaptive redundancy tuning.

FR-TEL-2: Apply adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission policies to outbound commands and inbound telemetry, with redundancy ratios and retry parameters configurable and tunable during commissioning.

FR-TEL-3: Differentiate message criticality (CRITICAL, NORMAL, LOW) based on explicit priority metadata in Tasking and Telemetry interfaces, and apply proportionate redundancy and queue prioritization accordingly.

FR-TEL-4: Detect and report packet loss threshold breaches (when actual loss exceeds customer-defined acceptance criterion) to SafetyAlert via explicit SafetyAlert interface signal, without enforcing safety decisions.

FR-TEL-5: Manage centralized configuration store for customer-defined packet loss acceptance criteria, redundancy ratios, and retry policies; support runtime updates and ensure consistency with SafetyAlert threshold reads.

revise.why: Recommended revisions to interface contracts (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link) and configuration management strategy should be incorporated into DP specifications before sub-level decomposition. Specifically: (1) Tasking and Telemetry interfaces must include explicit priority/criticality metadata fields; (2) Network Link interface must expose per-packet/per-flow QoS metrics with defined update frequency; (3) Configuration store design must be centralized and shared with SafetyAlert. These refinements will reduce ambiguity in sub-level DP derivation and ensure sub-FRs map to well-defined, implementable design parameters.

assumptions: Customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion (≤1% or equivalent target) is stable and known at commissioning time.
Network Link module provides real-time QoS metrics with sufficient granularity (per-packet or per-flow) and update frequency (e.g., ≥10 Hz) to support adaptive tuning.
Tasking and Telemetry interfaces can be extended to include priority/criticality metadata without breaking existing contracts.
SafetyAlert module is responsible for safety policy interpretation and enforcement; Teleoperation is responsible only for reporting raw metrics and threshold breaches.
Vehicle module does not implement redundancy logic for remote command/telemetry transport; redundancy is exclusively Teleoperation's responsibility.
Configuration store is accessible to both Teleoperation and SafetyAlert, and runtime updates are supported via a defined protocol (e.g., pub-sub, polling, or event-driven).
open_risks: MODERATE: Configuration consistency. If SafetyAlert and Teleoperation read packet loss thresholds from separate stores or at different times, threshold breach detection may be inconsistent. Mitigation: Centralize configuration store and define synchronization protocol.

MODERATE: Network metric freshness. If Network Link metrics are stale or infrequent, adaptive redundancy tuning may lag behind actual network conditions, causing transient packet loss spikes. Mitigation: Define minimum update frequency and metric precision in Network Link interface contract; validate during commissioning.

MINOR: Priority metadata propagation. If Tasking or Telemetry interfaces do not consistently include priority metadata, Teleoperation cannot reliably differentiate message criticality. Mitigation: Enforce priority metadata in interface contracts and validate at integration testing.

MINOR: SafetyAlert coordination latency. If SafetyAlert response to packet loss threshold breach is slow, operator may not be alerted in time to take corrective action. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert response time requirement and validate during commissioning.

MINOR: Commissioning tuning complexity. Iterative tuning of redundancy ratios to meet customer packet loss targets may be time-consuming and require domain expertise. Mitigation: Provide tuning guidance and automated baseline characterization tools.
evidence_needed: Interface contract specifications for Tasking, Telemetry, and Network Link, including priority/criticality metadata fields and QoS metric definitions.
Configuration store design document, including schema, access protocol, and synchronization mechanism with SafetyAlert.
SafetyAlert coordination protocol, defining how Teleoperation reports metrics and threshold breaches, and how SafetyAlert interprets and acts on them.
Commissioning procedure document, including network condition characterization, redundancy tuning workflow, and validation against acceptance criteria.
Vehicle module boundary clarification, confirming whether Vehicle owns any internal redundancy logic and establishing clear separation of concerns.
Sub-level DP derivations for FR-TEL-1 through FR-TEL-5, with explicit mapping to Teleoperation IM and external interfaces.
Verification plan for each sub-FR, including test cases, acceptance criteria, and measurement methods.
